_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Evangeline Parish, Louisiana. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for this piney woods-to-prairie transition parish created in 1910 from St. Landry. Evangeline is among the youngest parishes in Acadiana, meaning pre-1910 records are at St. Landry Parish in Opelousas. The parish straddles the cultural boundary between Cajun prairie and Anglo-Protestant piney woods.

current_courthouse.location: Ville Platte, LA -- 200 Court Street. Mid-century modern courthouse replacing the original.

previous_courthouses.location: Ville Platte -- original frame and brick courthouse built shortly after parish creation
previous_courthouses.fate: Served the parish for nearly fifty years before being replaced by the current courthouse. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.cause: Parish creation -- Evangeline was carved from St. Landry Parish in 1910
records_survived: All pre-1910 records remain at St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court, Opelousas
record_losses.description: Evangeline Parish was created in 1910 from the northern portion of St. Landry Parish. All civil records -- conveyances, successions, marriages, and court proceedings -- predating 1910 for the Evangeline area remain at the St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court in Opelousas. The Ville Platte area was part of the Opelousas district from the colonial period, and families in this region appear in St. Landry records from the 1780s forward.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in what is now Evangeline Parish before 1910, their civil records are at Opelousas in St. Landry -- one of the richest colonial-era record collections in southwestern Louisiana, but a different courthouse in a different town.
record_losses.source: Vidrine, Jacqueline O. Love's Legacy: The Mobile Marriages Recorded in French, Translated, with Annotated Abstracts in English, 1724-1786. Lafayette: Center for Louisiana Studies, 1985.

records_survived: All record series from 1910 forward appear intact
record_losses.description: Evangeline Parish has been fortunate in record preservation. The courthouse has not suffered fire, flood, or catastrophic storm damage since its creation. The primary challenge for researchers is not record loss but the late creation date of the parish, requiring research at St. Landry for all pre-1910 materials.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Evangeline Parish's records from 1910 forward are intact, but the late creation date means the researcher must navigate two courthouses -- Ville Platte for post-1910 and Opelousas for everything before.
record_losses.source: Fontenot, Mary Alice. Evangeline Parish. Charleston, SC: Arcadia Publishing, 2003.

deeds.completeness: Good -- continuous conveyance records from parish creation. Pre-1910 conveyances at St. Landry Parish.
deeds.gaps: No gaps in post-1910 records. Colonial-era land grants for this area are at St. Landry and at the Louisiana State Archives.
deeds.repository: Evangeline Parish Clerk of Court, Ville Platte

wills_estates.completeness: Good -- succession records continuous from parish creation
wills_estates.gaps: Pre-1910 successions at St. Landry Parish. Early Evangeline successions reflect a mixed economy of small farming, timber, and cattle.
wills_estates.repository: Evangeline Parish Clerk of Court, Ville Platte

marriages.completeness: Good from parish creation. Catholic church records from Sacred Heart at Ville Platte and other parishes supplement civil records from the 1870s.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1910 civil marriages at St. Landry Parish. Church sacramental records are earlier and more complete.
marriages.repository: Evangeline Parish Clerk of Court; Diocese of Lafayette archives
marriages.online_access: Some indexes through Louisiana Secretary of State

court_minutes.gaps: Pre-1910 court records at St. Landry Parish
court_minutes.repository: Evangeline Parish Clerk of Court, Ville Platte

vital_records.completeness: Fair -- statewide registration was in effect by 1910 but compliance in rural Evangeline Parish was uneven into the 1920s. Catholic church records fill many gaps.
vital_records.repository: Louisiana Office of Vital Records, New Orleans

substitute_sources.description: All civil records for the Evangeline area before 1910 reside at the St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court in Opelousas. This includes some of the richest colonial-era records in southwestern Louisiana -- Spanish colonial documents, French-language successions, and early American-period conveyances.
substitute_sources.repository: St. Landry Parish Clerk of Court, Opelousas, LA
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for all Evangeline Parish families before 1910. St. Landry was the mother parish for much of southwestern Louisiana, and its records document the original Acadian settlement, the free Creole of Color community, and the antebellum slave economy.
substitute_sources.source: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.

substitute_sources.description: Catholic churches in the Evangeline Parish area -- Sacred Heart at Ville Platte, St. Joseph at Pine Prairie, and churches in neighboring communities -- maintain baptism, marriage, and burial registers that predate the parish's civil records by decades.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual parish churches; Diocese of Lafayette archives
substitute_sources.era_coverage: 1870s-present (local churches); older records at neighboring parish churches
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Catholic records in Acadiana are frequently more detailed than civil records, particularly for the period before reliable civil vital registration. French-language entries document godparent networks and family connections.

substitute_sources.description: Jacqueline Vidrine and other local genealogists have compiled extensive records for the Ville Platte and Evangeline Parish area, drawing from church records, civil records, and family papers. These compilations are especially valuable for Creole of Color families in the parish.
substitute_sources.repository: Louisiana State Library; Evangeline Parish Library; LSU Special Collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Local compilations often include records that are difficult to access in their original form -- French-language church records, private notarial archives, and family Bible records collected from local families.
substitute_sources.source: Vidrine, Jacqueline O. Love's Legacy. Lafayette: Center for Louisiana Studies, 1985.

narrative_hooks: Evangeline Parish did not exist until 1910 -- if {ancestor_name} lived in the Ville Platte area before then, their records are at Opelousas in St. Landry Parish, written in French, under a legal system inherited from Napoleon.

The piney woods-to-prairie transition that defined Evangeline Parish also defined its people -- {ancestor_name}'s family was Cajun if they farmed the prairie edge, but might have been Anglo-Protestant if they worked the timber in the piney woods to the north.

Catholic church records at Ville Platte name every parrain and marraine at {ancestor_name}'s baptism -- godparents chosen with care to cement alliances between families in a community where kinship was everything.

If {ancestor_name} was part of the gens de couleur libres community in the Ville Platte area, the church records and notarial archives may document a free Creole of Color family history stretching back to the Spanish colonial period.
source_references: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. 39 vols. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.
Fontenot, Mary Alice. Evangeline Parish. Charleston, SC: Arcadia Publishing, 2003.
Vidrine, Jacqueline O. Love's Legacy: The Mobile Marriages Recorded in French, Translated, with Annotated Abstracts in English, 1724-1786. Lafayette: Center for Louisiana Studies, 1985.
Brasseaux, Carl A. Acadian to Cajun: Transformation of a People, 1803-1877. Jackson: University Press of Mississippi, 1992.
